content.text: Knowledge distillation transfers the learned representations of a large, expensive model — the teacher — into a smaller, faster model — the student. Rather than training the student directly on one-hot labels (which discard all the nuance the teacher learned), distillation trains the student to reproduce the teacher's full output distribution, including the relative probabilities assigned to incorrect but semantically related tokens. This soft supervision encodes richer information than hard labels alone.

The teacher-student framework consists of two models operating in tandem during training. The teacher is a large pretrained model whose weights are frozen — it acts purely as an inference engine, generating soft probability distributions over the vocabulary for each input. The student is a smaller model being trained: it receives the same inputs and must learn to produce similar output distributions through gradient descent. The teacher provides richer supervision than ground-truth labels because its probability distribution reflects genuine model uncertainty and semantic relationships between tokens.

The framework was introduced by Hinton, Vinyals, and Dean (2015) for classification tasks, where it was shown that a student trained on teacher soft labels could match the performance of a student trained with ensemble methods at a fraction of the cost. The core insight — called 'dark knowledge' — is that the probabilities a model assigns to incorrect classes encode structural information about which classes are similar to each other, information that is invisible in a one-hot label.

Large language models (GPT-4, Llama-3 70B, Claude) achieve remarkable performance but are too expensive to deploy in many settings — inference on a 70B model requires multiple high-end GPUs and returns latency of hundreds of milliseconds per token. Knowledge distillation provides a principled way to compress these capabilities into a 1B–7B parameter model that runs on a single GPU or consumer hardware, while retaining much of the teacher's performance on downstream tasks.

The economic argument is compelling: a 70B teacher model run once to generate soft labels for a large training set can produce supervision data for a 1B student that, after distillation, performs comparably to the 70B teacher on specific tasks. At inference time, the 1B student is 50–100× cheaper to run than the 70B teacher.

Modern small language models — Microsoft's Phi series, Google's Gemma, Meta's Llama-3 1B — are trained using large amounts of distillation data (synthetic outputs from larger models), which is why they achieve performance far above what their parameter count would suggest if trained on raw internet text alone.
A 1B student performs similarly to a 7B teacher on many tasks, at 7× lower inference cost (fewer FLOPs, less memory, faster throughput).

Soft labels carry semantic similarity information invisible in one-hot targets — if the teacher assigns 30% to 'cat' and 60% to 'dog', the student learns that cats and dogs are more similar to each other than to 'automobile'.
Distillation often requires less real labeled data than training from scratch because the teacher's outputs serve as dense, high-quality supervision even on unlabeled inputs.
The student can use a completely different architecture from the teacher — different depth, width, attention type, or context window — as long as it shares the same vocabulary.
Distillation on a specific task distribution produces a student that compresses the teacher's capabilities relevant to that task, without carrying the cost of general-purpose capability.
The student is bounded by the teacher's performance. A 1B student trained via distillation from a 7B teacher cannot exceed the 7B teacher on the tasks it was distilled on.

Distillation requires running the teacher model for every training example, often once per forward pass. For a 70B teacher and a dataset of 100B tokens, teacher inference alone can take weeks on a GPU cluster.

The student learns from the teacher's distribution on the training set. If the student encounters inputs at inference time that differ from the training distribution, it may not inherit the teacher's capabilities there.
The combined distillation + CE loss introduces hyperparameters (temperature T, distillation weight α) that must be tuned, adding a calibration step before production training.
Precomputing and caching teacher logits for a large dataset requires significant disk space — storing float16 logits over a 32k vocabulary for 100M tokens requires roughly 6TB of storage.

The key innovation in distillation is temperature scaling. Before computing probabilities, the teacher's (and student's) raw logits are divided by a temperature parameter T > 1. This flattens the probability distribution, making low-probability tokens more visible and giving the student a richer training signal.

With T=1 (standard softmax), a model that is very confident assigns nearly all probability to one token. With T=4, the same logits produce a much smoother distribution where the second and third most likely tokens receive non-trivial probability mass. This soft distribution is what the student learns to mimic.

The T² factor compensates for the fact that dividing logits by T reduces the magnitude of the gradients flowing back through the softmax — multiplying by T² restores the original gradient magnitude. Without this correction, increasing T would reduce the effective learning rate of the distillation objective.
The full combined loss mixes the distillation loss with the standard cross-entropy loss against ground-truth labels:

Typical values are T=2–4 and α=0.5–0.9. Higher α weights the distillation signal more heavily than the ground-truth labels, which is appropriate when the teacher model is more accurate than the human-labelled ground truth (common for specialized reasoning tasks).

attrs.alt: Teacher-student knowledge distillation pipeline: both teacher (large, frozen) and student (small, trainable) receive the same input; teacher produces soft logits with temperature scaling; KL divergence between teacher and student distributions plus cross-entropy to ground truth forms the combined loss that backpropagates through the student only

content.text: Standard distillation uses forward KL divergence: KL(p_teacher || p_student). Forward KL is 'mean-seeking' — to avoid infinite loss, the student must assign non-zero probability wherever the teacher does, which encourages the student to cover all modes of the teacher's distribution. This is appropriate when we want the student to generalize broadly.

Some modern LLM distillation work uses reverse KL: KL(p_student || p_teacher). Reverse KL is 'mode-seeking' — it penalizes the student for assigning probability where the teacher does not, which encourages the student to focus on the teacher's high-probability regions rather than spreading probability mass thinly. Reverse KL produces students that are more peaked and more likely to match the teacher's most confident outputs, but may underperform on rare or ambiguous cases.
Mean-seeking; student covers all teacher modes
Mode-seeking; student sharpens on teacher peaks
Symmetric; bounded; less extreme than either
When teacher and student architectures differ greatly

Knowledge distillation is the right choice when you have a high-performing teacher model and need to serve predictions at lower cost or latency. Concretely, the typical triggers are: a capable 7B–70B model has been developed or fine-tuned but inference costs are prohibitive for production; the target device constrains model size (edge deployment, mobile); or you need to compress capabilities learned through expensive RLHF into a smaller base.

Distillation is less appropriate when the teacher is only marginally better than publicly available smaller models (the gap is too small to recover via distillation), when the student architecture is so different from the teacher that vocabulary alignment is impossible, or when compute budget for teacher inference is tighter than the inference budget that triggered the need for compression.
Step 1: Set Up Teacher and Student Models

import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

TEACHER_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"
STUDENT_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.2-1B"  # same tokenizer, smaller model

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(TEACHER_ID)

# Teacher: loaded in inference mode, frozen
teacher = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    TEACHER_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="cuda:0",
)
for p in teacher.parameters():
    p.requires_grad = False
teacher.eval()

# Student: loaded for training
student = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    STUDENT_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="cuda:1",   # separate GPU if available
)
student.train()

import torch.nn.functional as F

def distillation_loss(
    student_logits: torch.Tensor,   # (batch, seq_len, vocab_size)
    teacher_logits: torch.Tensor,   # (batch, seq_len, vocab_size)
    labels: torch.Tensor,           # (batch, seq_len) — ground-truth token ids
    temperature: float = 3.0,
    alpha: float = 0.7,
    ignore_index: int = -100,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Combined cross-entropy + KL distillation loss for causal LM."""
    # Flatten batch and sequence dimensions
    B, S, V = student_logits.shape
    s_logits = student_logits.reshape(-1, V).float()  # cast to fp32 for loss
    t_logits = teacher_logits.reshape(-1, V).float()
    flat_labels = labels.reshape(-1)

    # Standard cross-entropy loss on ground-truth tokens
    ce_loss = F.cross_entropy(s_logits, flat_labels, ignore_index=ignore_index)

    # Temperature-scaled KL divergence (only on non-padding positions)
    mask = flat_labels != ignore_index
    s_log_probs = F.log_softmax(s_logits[mask] / temperature, dim=-1)
    t_probs = F.softmax(t_logits[mask] / temperature, dim=-1)
    kl = F.kl_div(s_log_probs, t_probs, reduction="batchmean")

    return alpha * (temperature ** 2) * kl + (1 - alpha) * ce_loss

from transformers import DataCollatorForLanguageModeling, get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from datasets import load_from_disk

dataset = load_from_disk("data/distillation_packed")
dataloader = DataLoader(
    dataset, batch_size=4, shuffle=True,
    collate_fn=DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)
)

optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(student.parameters(), lr=1e-4, weight_decay=0.01)
scheduler = get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup(optimizer, num_warmup_steps=200, num_training_steps=10_000)

for step, batch in enumerate(dataloader):
    input_ids = batch["input_ids"].to("cuda:1")
    labels = batch["labels"].to("cuda:1")

    # Student forward pass
    student_out = student(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)

    # Teacher forward pass (no grad, may be on different GPU)
    with torch.no_grad():
        teacher_out = teacher(input_ids=input_ids.to("cuda:0"))
    t_logits = teacher_out.logits.to("cuda:1")  # move to student device

    loss = distillation_loss(
        student_logits=student_out.logits,
        teacher_logits=t_logits,
        labels=labels,
        temperature=3.0,
        alpha=0.7,
    )

    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(student.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    optimizer.step()
    scheduler.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()

    if step % 100 == 0:
        print(f"Step {step}: loss={loss.item():.4f}")

Running the teacher at every training step doubles the compute cost of each step. For large-scale distillation, it is far more efficient to precompute and cache the teacher's logits for the entire training dataset before the student training loop begins. This converts the teacher cost from O(training steps) to O(dataset size), and decouples teacher compute from student GPU requirements.

"""precompute_teacher.py — generate and cache teacher logits for distillation."""
import torch, json
from pathlib import Path
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from datasets import load_from_disk, Dataset
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, DataCollatorForLanguageModeling

teacher = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto")
teacher.eval()

dataset = load_from_disk("data/distillation_packed")
loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=8, shuffle=False)

all_logits = []
with torch.no_grad():
    for i, batch in enumerate(loader):
        ids = batch["input_ids"].to("cuda")
        logits = teacher(input_ids=ids).logits  # (B, S, V)
        # Keep only top-k logits per position to save space
        TOP_K = 64  # store only top 64 token logits (99.9%+ probability mass)
        top_vals, top_idx = logits.topk(TOP_K, dim=-1)  # (B, S, 64)
        all_logits.append({
            "values": top_vals.half().cpu().numpy().tolist(),
            "indices": top_idx.short().cpu().numpy().tolist(),
        })
        if i % 100 == 0:
            print(f"Processed {i * 8} examples")

# Save as a HuggingFace dataset for efficient streaming
logit_ds = Dataset.from_list(all_logits)
logit_ds.save_to_disk("data/teacher_logits")

Distillation was originally designed for classification models where a single distribution over classes is produced per example. Autoregressive LLMs produce one distribution per token in the sequence, making the distillation problem much larger in scale but structurally similar: for each position in the sequence, the student learns to match the teacher's next-token distribution.

Token-level distillation is the standard approach: compute the KL divergence at each non-padding token position and average across all positions and batch elements. This is equivalent to training the student to match the teacher's full conditional distribution p(x_t | x_1, ..., x_{t-1}) at each step.

Sequence-level distillation is an alternative where the student is trained to generate the teacher's most likely output sequence (beam search output) rather than matching the teacher's distribution at each step. This is simpler to implement but discards most of the rich distributional signal — it is essentially training on teacher-generated labels rather than true distillation.

A production team needs a model that can summarize customer support tickets. The 8B Llama-3 model achieves good performance but the latency requirement (< 200ms per summary, 4096 input tokens) demands a 1B parameter model. Direct fine-tuning of the 1B model on the summarization dataset underperforms: the 1B model's training loss reaches the same value as the 8B model but ROUGE-L scores are 8 points lower.

The distillation approach: run the 8B model to generate token-level logits for 2M summarization training examples (takes 18 hours on 4 × A100). Train the 1B student on these logits with T=3.0 and α=0.8. The distilled 1B model reaches ROUGE-L scores within 3 points of the 8B teacher while meeting the latency constraint.

# Evaluation comparison after distillation
from evaluate import load
rouge = load("rouge")

# 8B teacher (inference-only baseline)
teacher_summaries = generate_summaries(teacher, test_articles)
teacher_scores = rouge.compute(predictions=teacher_summaries, references=references)
print(f"Teacher 8B - ROUGE-L: {teacher_scores['rougeL']:.3f}")
# → Teacher 8B - ROUGE-L: 0.441

# 1B student (directly fine-tuned, no distillation)
baseline_summaries = generate_summaries(baseline_1b, test_articles)
baseline_scores = rouge.compute(predictions=baseline_summaries, references=references)
print(f"Baseline 1B - ROUGE-L: {baseline_scores['rougeL']:.3f}")
# → Baseline 1B - ROUGE-L: 0.362

# 1B student (distilled from 8B)
distilled_summaries = generate_summaries(distilled_1b, test_articles)
distilled_scores = rouge.compute(predictions=distilled_summaries, references=references)
print(f"Distilled 1B - ROUGE-L: {distilled_scores['rougeL']:.3f}")
# → Distilled 1B - ROUGE-L: 0.409  (bridged 65% of the gap)
Teacher distribution too peaked; student sees almost one-hot supervision, losing dark knowledge benefit
Use T=2–4; typical start is T=3
Distillation loss gradient 10–100× smaller than expected; student ignores teacher signal
Always include T² factor in KL loss term
KL divergence between misaligned distributions is meaningless
Teacher and student must share the same tokenizer
Teacher updates during student training; distribution shifts unpredictably
Set requires_grad=False for all teacher parameters; call teacher.eval()
No knowledge transfer — equivalent to fine-tuning on labels only
Set α > 0; α=0.5–0.9 is the productive range
Training only on distillation data from one task
Student overfits to task distribution; loses general capability
Mix diverse distillation data or include replay corpus
Padding token positions inflate the KL loss with meaningless signal
Mask padding positions before computing KL divergence
Run a small sweep over T ∈ {2, 3, 4} before committing to the full training run. Different tasks have different optimal temperatures depending on how peaked the teacher's distributions are.
A 70% distillation / 30% CE split is a reliable default. Increase α toward 1.0 if the teacher is known to be more accurate than the ground-truth labels.
Teacher and student must use an identical vocabulary and tokenizer. Even minor differences in special token handling can break the token-level alignment.
Precompute teacher logits for large datasets:
For datasets above 1M examples, precomputing and caching teacher logits is almost always more efficient than online teacher inference during training.
Inspect a few teacher soft distributions to confirm they have meaningful probability spread. If the teacher assigns >99% to one token at most positions (T=1 behavior), increase T.
Monitor student perplexity and task metrics:
Track both the distillation loss and downstream task metrics. A decreasing distillation loss doesn't guarantee downstream improvement — evaluate both.

Teacher inference is typically the bottleneck in large-scale distillation. A 70B teacher model can generate logits for roughly 500–1000 tokens/second per GPU. Distilling from a 70B teacher on a 100B token dataset requires approximately 100,000–200,000 GPU-hours of teacher inference alone — an important cost to budget before committing to distillation at this scale.

Storing full teacher logits for a 100B token dataset at float16 over a 32k vocabulary requires 6.4TB of disk space. The top-k logit trick (storing only the highest-k token probabilities per position) reduces this dramatically: with k=64, storing float16 values and int16 indices requires only 100B × 64 × (2+2) bytes ≈ 25GB — manageable on standard storage.

Training the student with cached logits is efficient: the student forward+backward pass is not bottlenecked by the teacher, and the dataloader reads cached logits in parallel with training. Student training throughput with cached logits is essentially the same as standard fine-tuning throughput.
High-level API; handles logit caching and KL loss
Research framework for LLM distillation experiments
Flexible; supports feature-level and output-level distillation
Use reduction='batchmean'; ensure log-softmax on student side
Generate synthetic distillation data via LLM APIs
Uses teacher LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) to generate training labels
Same model, reduced memory at inference
Smaller dense model from large model

A distilled student model can inherit not only the teacher's capabilities but also its failure modes, biases, and safety behaviors. If the teacher produces biased outputs on sensitive topics, the student trained to mimic the teacher's distribution will reproduce those biases. If the teacher is a safety-tuned RLHF model, the student trained purely on distillation loss may partially recover unsafe behaviors present in the base model that the teacher's RLHF training suppressed — because the distillation objective optimizes distribution matching, not safety alignment.

Always evaluate the distilled student independently for safety and bias using the same test suites applied to the teacher. Do not assume that distillation transfers safety properties — verify them explicitly. Include a safety-aware component in the distillation loss (e.g., a penalty when the student assigns high probability to flagged outputs) if safety preservation is a requirement.

The best intuition for distillation is apprenticeship. A junior chef (student) doesn't just learn from recipe cards (hard labels) — they watch a master chef (teacher) work and internalize the proportions, timing, and judgment calls the master makes at every step. The recipe says 'add salt' but the master's hand (soft distribution) reveals how much salt, adjusted for the specific ingredients at hand, in a way no recipe can fully capture. Temperature scaling is like slowing down the master's hand to make subtle gestures visible — a T=1 master moves too fast to observe nuance; a T=3 master's every hesitation and micro-adjustment is legible. The student learns from this richer signal and surpasses what the recipe alone could teach.

Knowledge distillation trains a small student model to reproduce a large teacher model's output probability distribution, not just its final predictions — this 'soft supervision' carries far more information than one-hot labels.
Temperature scaling (T=2–4) flattens the teacher's distribution, making low-probability tokens visible; the T² correction factor in the loss preserves gradient magnitude.
The combined loss L = α·T²·KL(p_teacher || p_student) + (1-α)·L_CE mixes distillation and ground-truth supervision; α=0.7 and T=3 are reliable starting points.
Teacher and student must share the same tokenizer and vocabulary — mismatched vocabularies make token-level distribution matching undefined.
For large datasets (>1M examples), precompute and cache teacher logits before student training; storing only top-64 logits per position reduces storage by 500× with minimal signal loss.
Distillation can transfer task performance but does NOT automatically transfer safety alignment — the student must be independently evaluated for safety.
Forward KL (mean-seeking) is the standard choice for general-purpose distillation; reverse KL (mode-seeking) suits task-specific compression where precision matters more than coverage.
Modern small models (Phi-3, Gemma-2B) owe much of their above-expected performance to distillation from larger models during pretraining data curation.
